chapters.title: Big Bang, or how the universe began
chapters.text:
Every person in the world once asked themselves how the universe began. Some of our ancestors considered that it dated approximately 5000 BC. Of course, archaeological findings proved this suggestion wrong, as only Earth formed 4.5 billion years ago. Greek philosophers believed that the universe existed for an infinite amount of time and would last forever.

Astronomer Edwin Hubble made one of the most remarkable discoveries in the field in 1929. He noticed that galaxies move in the opposite direction from us, implying that the universe expands. Moreover, scientists suggested that they accumulated in one place back then, which brought us to the development of the Big Bang cosmological model.

There are, however, more popular theories, like Isaac Newton's theory of gravity and Albert Einstein's general theory of relativity. Nowadays, scientists apply both of them to interpret the world. While Einstein's approach characterizes the universe on the macro scale, quantum mechanics apply to a micro level. Yet, these two theories contradict each other. One of the crucial tasks for Stephen Hawking was to find a way to combine both sets of principles. His search started with the analysis of space and time models.

Even if you think you are lazy just resting on the bed, in reality, you cross space due to the Earth's rotation. Everything is indeed relative in this world.

Join this expedition into the universe. Hawking has described how the latter looks, why objects act in certain ways, and how physics developed over time. While exploring these topics, you will realize the power of human intellect and the world's complexity. Physics is a highly counterintuitive field, so get ready to be astonished.

chapters.title: Sky (read "universe") is the limit
chapters.text:
Einstein's legendary equation E=mc2 demonstrated that the more energy the object receives while moving, the more mass it will gain. Therefore, it cannot increase its speed at some point due to elevated mass. Other Einstein's revolutionary findings indicated that the time-space continuum is warped, so light does not move straight. Its direction can be changed by massive objects causing its deflection.

Imagine dropping a stone in the sand. It will create an indentation. Now you may shine a torch into the rock, and you will see how the light bends into the dint. It does the same in space, curving due to the planet's weight.

Consequently, massive objects also slow down time flow. This effect rests on the principle that lesser light energy leads to its lower frequency, which happens when it needs to move up against gravity. Recall the cases when you spend a vacation in the high mountains. Did you feel that time flies? It is not only because you were on holiday. Minutes ticked away faster there.

In 1962, an experiment proved it. Scientists put one clock at the bottom of the water tower and another at its top. As a result, the device placed higher ran faster. This principle is fundamental for the work of new navigation systems. If they disregard it, the signals from satellites would not be so correct.

Newton's and Einstein's discoveries defined time and space as dynamic quantities.

What about the perception of color? We distinguish between hues because they have diverse wavelengths. Long waves are seen as the red part of the color spectrum, while short ones are in the blue segment. Using this knowledge, we can observe the expansion of the universe.

In the case of light, stars moving away from us will have their spectra shifted toward the red end of the spectrum, which is called red-shifted, and those moving toward us will have their spectra blueshifted. As Stephen Hawking has explained, this is how we measure cosmic motion.

American astronomer Edwin Hubble estimated that almost all the galaxies around us are in the red part of the specter, meaning they are moving away. This conclusion rests on the Doppler effect. It states that the waves' frequency increases as the source and observer move toward each other and vice versa. For example, to estimate a car's speed, police use specific devices that calculate the length of radio waves emitted by the vehicle passing by.

chapters.title: Fortune-tellers are liars!
chapters.text:
Alexander Friedmann, a Soviet physicist, predicted that the universe looks almost the same in any direction, which Penzias and Wilson later proved. These two American physicists discovered that a specific microwave noise was coming from outside our galaxy. This finding means that we receive light from the farthest parts of the cosmos, which reaches us now because of the universe's expansion. Besides, scientists concluded that, most likely, the latter will never cease because there is not enough gravity to influence the movement of an enormous mass of all objects in space.

Max Planck, a theoretical physicist, suggested that different waves only emit a specific amount of energy that increases with their frequency. This assumption became the foundation for Heisenberg's uncertainty principle. It states that it is impossible to predict a particle's position and speed exactly. To find the former, we need to illuminate, for instance, the electron so that it reflects the light. However, the latter's wavelength affects the particle's speed. The shorter it is, the higher energy it contributes to the electron. Therefore, we will not know its speed and position simultaneously and never determine its future location.

This discovery demonstrated that our world could not be deterministic. Heisenberg's findings also suggested that particles can behave as waves. A two-slit experiment proved it. Imagine directing a wave of water on a board with two closely spaced slits and a piece of paper behind. After passing the board, it will leave a long mark on the sheet, showing that it passed through both slits since waves expand while moving. Now imagine that you have to drop a water droplet through one of the slits. The mark on the paper will show only one wet spot.

But that is not how quantum mechanics works. Even if the researcher shoots only one electron towards the partition with two slits and the screen behind, the latter will show that the particle behaved as a wave. Notably, it crossed both slits simultaneously.

This discovery was essential for explaining the atom's structure. Scientists used to believe it resembled the solar system, with electrons orbiting around a central nucleus. Yet this model contained a flaw, since it meant that particles would lose energy. The solution was to consider electrons' moves as waves.

Everything affects everything, and we cannot predict future events because we cannot even foretell the location of one single particle.

chapters.title: There are parts even smaller than atoms
chapters.text:
Gell-Mann's proof that protons and neutrons are not the smallest particles was revolutionary. By making them collide, he found out that they consisted of quarks. Scientists divide them into six categories, which are top, down, up, charmed, strange, and bottom.

Protons and neutrons comprise three quarks each.

Someone who looks exactly like you may be your antiparticle copy.

Even more exciting, each particle has its antipode. For instance, an electron's one is called a positron. Yet the meeting of opposites results in destruction. Thus, we may suppose that anti-We, created from antiparticles, exist somewhere on earth. Yet we should never touch them, since they will turn us into a flash of light.

A prognosis states that a proton can turn into an antielectron. This transformation occurs if one of the three quarks inside the former gains more energy to become the latter. Yet it happens incredibly rarely, once in 1 followed by 30 zeros years.

We also know that galaxies consisting of quarks and antiquarks cannot exist. These particles would annihilate, leaving considerable radiation we could have detected. It is fascinating why we now have more quarks than antiquarks in the universe. How did this happen.

Even if their proportions were the same initially, it would still lead to this dominance. Why? High temperatures initially empowered quarks, antiquarks, antielectrons, and electrons with colossal energy, allowing their transformation. Yet the physics laws affect various types of particles differently. According to them, the universe's development influenced more antielectrons, causing them to change into quarks.

This inequality has benefited us enormously. Without it, opposites would destroy themselves, and instead of matter, we would have only radiation. It means the cosmos had no stars, earth, or humanity.

Now let us consider the appearance of black holes. They do not randomly occur at some moment in space. When a star ages, it begins to condense, and therefore its gravitational force grows. Finally, the gravitational field increases to the point when the light cannot escape at all. If the fastest mover in the universe cannot do it, nothing else will get away. Thus, a black hole appears. The circle of light around it is called the event horizon.

chapters.title: Black holes not only catch but lose, too
chapters.text:
At some point in examining black holes, Hawking concluded that they emit radiation like any hot object in the universe. Yet, how is it possible that even light cannot escape their boundaries? As it turned out, radiation appears not inside the black hole but in the area outside the event horizon. It contains so-called virtual particles, which our devices cannot detect. After entering a black hole, they can drop out of its area as antiparticles or real particles.

As Stephen Hawking has described, the smaller the black hole, the shorter the distance the particle with negative energy will have to go before it becomes a real particle, and thus the greater the rate of emission, and the apparent temperature, of the black hole.

This finding means that there is a way out of a black hole. For example, if a person enters it, its mass will increase. Then, this addition returns to space as radiation, in which human particles transform.

There is a way out, even from a black hole, so do not be discouraged by your earthly troubles.

As for the universe's formation based on the Big Bang theory, after the universe's temperature fell and equaled that inside the stars, neutrons and protons started to combine into nuclei. The first ones created were deuterium, which formed helium and other elements. George Gamow, a theoretical physicist, and his student Ralph Alpher, a cosmologist, described this process.

As witty as he was, Gamow convinced another scientist, Hans Bethe, a nuclear physicist who did not participate in writing this work, into adding his name to the article. The combination of their last names, Alpher, Bethe, and Gamow, reflected the alphabet letters alpha, beta, and gamma. They predicted Penzias' and Wilson's finding of radiation from the furthest reaches of the universe.

Early-formed chemical elements made the first stars. When they exploded, their wreckage scattered over the universe, creating planets and other astronomical objects. Alan Guth, an American theoretical physicist, supposed that cosmos initially developed at an unthinkable rate. Imagine one followed by 30 zeros. That is how the space extended in less than a second. Its temperature was scorching, and protons, neutrons, and so on quickly moved in different directions. While developing, the universe became cooler, and particles' energy decreased.

For a small-scale reference, scientist Jeff Steinhauer and his colleagues created a mini-version of a black hole on Earth in 2009.

chapters.title: The possibilities of time travel
chapters.text:
At some point in your life, you might wonder if we can turn back time and ensure the objects get into their previous form. According to the second law of thermodynamics, entropy grows over time. Simply put, the latter means a disordered condition, some randomness. For instance, breaking a cup moves it to entropy from the past state of order. But you cannot unbreak it.

We can divide time into three arrows or directions. One is entropy, another is our perception of time, and the last is the universe's expansion. The number of chaotic configurations in the cosmos is much bigger than ordered ones. The analogy with a cup will explain this better. We can break it differently, since its handle or the bottom will fall out. Yet there is only one chance for it to stay in order, which is if we do not damage it.

Science fiction writers enjoy fantasizing about navigating galaxies. They suppose that people will someday figure out how to move faster than the speed of light. In reality, space tourists will feel they spent little time out there compared to those who wait on earth. They will likely come back a bit older just to discover that all their friends died many years ago. However, if such travelers could bend space-time to connect two points in space, they would create a wormhole. It would allow us to reach Alpha Centauri as if it was just as close as the moon is to earth. Moreover, the wormholes' existence would also allow time travel.

The structure of space-time was also a focal point of string theories. It analyzes one-dimensional extended entities, smaller than atoms, electrons, or quarks, and calls them strings. They can be open and closed. If two closed ones unite, they will form something similar to a pair of trousers. They interact with different forces, which causes their fluctuations.

However, these theories have a vital condition, since such strings can only exist if there are 10 or 26 dimensions. We have only discovered four so far, but researchers believe others are visible on tiny scales. Thus, we cannot detect them at the moment. Nevertheless, we will not be able to travel through these dimensions since they are too small.

Even though faith in space-time travels remains, modern theories illustrate the extreme complexity of realizing such dreams.

chapters.title: Why is string theory so important?
chapters.text:
Strings gained little popularity at first. However, in 1984, researchers began to scrutinize them. While working on the supergravity theory as a part of string one, they realized they could not prove that it was finite yet. They also discovered that strings could clarify the reasons for specific particles' existence, so they based a heterotic theory on them. The new one could interpret different particles that scientists examine. Since it only worked for tiny scales, researchers must continue searching to develop a more consistent theory. But why are they so interested in this incompatible-with-reality set of principles anyway.

The existence of additional dimensions allows us to get around the limits of general relativity, which state that we cannot move back in time and faster than light. String theory tolerates an alternative route through other dimensions. Imagine a figure similar to a doughnut but flat because it exists in a universe with only length and width. You are on the right side of the pastry's inner circle. How do you get to the left side? Since you are also two-dimensional, you will have to follow a trajectory similar to an arc. But if you had a third dimension, you could jump straight to the left side.

String theory aims to provide us with the shortest possible route through space. Hawking suggested that the universe's dimensions were all curved initially. However, our four ones became flat, probably due to an anthropic principle. For living creatures to develop, a two-dimensional universe would not be convenient enough. They would have to move each other up to get where they needed to go. Besides, they could not eat, since the gastrointestinal tract would simply divide them into parts.

Never stop being curious about the world around you. That is what makes life so exciting.

Planets and stars would not be possible either. Gravitational force in a two-dimensional cosmos will not allow the Sun to exist due to gravitational pressure. It would likely turn into a black hole, meaning that life on earth would not even have a chance to begin. On a smaller scale, the situation will not be good either. Electrons will not orbit the protons due to gravitational pressure. They would likely fall apart, so we would not know atoms as they are in a four-dimensional world.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
As you can see, everything is ambiguous in the universe. Scientists try to connect all the dots regarding our discoveries about the world to see how it really looks. However, the more we find, the more questions arise. Why do quants behave weirdly? We have much to explore, but is it not an incredible journey.

Hawking spent a significant part of his life examining black holes. First, he thought they could not emit anything because of enormous gravitation, which trapped everything inside. Later, he realized that some particles could leave a black hole. Physics is a magical science that makes you question everything you find. It is all about uncertainty.

Usually, we are shocked when people say that they have figured out everything about themselves and their future. And we feel insecure about ourselves. What is wrong with me? Why cannot I understand what I need in my life? Do not worry about these feelings. These are quants that bring uncertainty into our lives. Unpredictability is the universe itself. People who are now sure about something were or will be utterly unsettled about something else in their past or future.

Try this. First, watch educational videos to add visual experiences to your discovery, since you will gain a fuller understanding of how physics laws work. Second, find out how people check physics' laws experimentally and try to do some. For instance, you can make a coin vanish due to light refraction by following the instructions on iop.org. However, avoid checking how black holes work at home, since we do not want you to turn into a flash of light. Third, start watching the popular The Big Bang Theory series about the lives of professional scientists. Steven Hawking starred in some episodes.

Read it till the end?
